Mechanistic Interpretability Research

The "Aha!" Moment: Understanding Grokking in AI

Investigating how a one-layer transformer suddenly learns to generalize on modular arithmetic — long after memorizing the training data — through the lens of mechanistic interpretability.

📐 Modular Arithmetic (mod 97) 🧠 One-Layer Transformer 🔬 Fourier Circuit Analysis 🔗 PyTorch
Overview Architecture 1 · Grokking 2 · Fourier 3 · Phases 4 · Data Fraction 5 · Alignment 6 · Multiplication 7 · Co-Grokking References

Project Overview

Grokking is a striking phenomenon in machine learning where a model suddenly learns to generalize long after it has seemingly overfit the training data. In a typical training scenario, once a model achieves near-zero training error while validation error remains high, we assume it has simply memorized the noise. However, if training continues well past this point of apparent convergence, the validation accuracy can suddenly "click" and shoot up to near-perfect levels.

This project investigates grokking using a one-layer transformer trained on modular arithmetic tasks (mod 97), following the methodology of Nanda et al. (2023). We analyze the process through mechanistic interpretability — reverse-engineering the model's internal weights and activations to identify the human-understandable algorithm it learns.

1Reproduce GrokkingTrain/val loss curves on modular addition 2Fourier AnalysisDFT of embeddings reveals sparse key frequencies 3Three PhasesMemorization → Circuit Formation → Cleanup 4Data FractionGrokking only in specific data regimes 5Alignment EssayAI safety meets mechanistic interpretability 6MultiplicationReverse-engineering mod multiplication 7Co-GrokkingMulti-task simultaneous generalization

Model Architecture

We use a custom one-layer transformer matching the architecture from Nanda et al., implemented from scratch in PyTorch (no TransformerLens dependency). The model has no LayerNorm and uses learned positional embeddings.

ParameterValueDescription
p (prime)97Modulus for arithmetic
d_model128Model/residual stream dimension
n_heads4Attention heads
d_head32Per-head dimension
d_mlp512MLP hidden units
Vocabulary980–96 (numbers) + = sign
Total params223,201All trainable parameters

Training: AdamW optimizer with lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1.0, betas=(0.9, 0.98). Full-batch gradient descent for 40,000 epochs. The aggressive weight decay is critical — it's what eventually prunes the memorization circuit.

Data: All $p^2 = 9{,}409$ pairs $(a, b)$ are generated. 30% are used for training (2,822 pairs), 70% for testing (6,587 pairs).

Component 1 · 10 marks

Reproducing Grokking

We train the transformer on modular addition: given input tokens $[a, b, =]$, predict $(a + b) \bmod 97$. The characteristic grokking signature emerges clearly:

  • Within ~1,000 epochs, training accuracy reaches 100% — the model has memorized all 2,822 training examples.
  • Test accuracy remains at chance level (~1%) for thousands more epochs — the model has not generalized.
  • Around epoch 20,000–30,000, test accuracy suddenly jumps to near-perfect — the "aha moment."
Run Component 1 to generate: training/validation loss and accuracy curves showing the dramatic grokking transition.
Figure 1 — Training and validation loss/accuracy curves for modular addition (mod 97). The gap between memorization and generalization is the hallmark of grokking.

This result demonstrates that perfect training accuracy does not imply understanding. The model first memorizes the data through brute-force, then — driven by weight decay — discovers the underlying mathematical structure.

Component 2 · +5 marks

Fourier Analysis of Embeddings

Applying a 1D Discrete Fourier Transform to the learned embedding matrix $W_E$ and MLP post-ReLU activations reveals the "clock algorithm":

The Clock Algorithm: The model maps each number $n$ to a point on circles at specific frequencies $\omega_k$: $n \mapsto (\cos(2\pi \omega_k n / p), \sin(2\pi \omega_k n / p))$. Addition becomes rotation on these circles, computed via the trigonometric identity: $\cos(\omega(a+b)) = \cos(\omega a)\cos(\omega b) - \sin(\omega a)\sin(\omega b)$.

The Fourier spectrum is remarkably sparse — only a handful of frequencies (out of 48 possible) carry significant power. This sparsity is the signature of a clean, generalizable algorithm rather than memorization.

Run Component 2 to generate: Fourier power spectra of W_E and MLP activations, plus heatmaps showing frequency sparsity.
Figure 2 — Fourier analysis showing sparse key frequencies in the embedding matrix and MLP activations. Red bars indicate frequencies with >10% of maximum power.
Component 3 · +5 marks

Three Phases of Grokking

By tracking restricted loss (using only key Fourier frequencies), excluded loss (using only non-key frequencies), and weight norm, we decompose grokking into three continuous phases:

① Memorization

Excluded loss drops sharply as the model builds a memorization circuit using non-generalizing frequencies. Training loss → 0, test loss stays high.

② Circuit Formation

Restricted loss drops as the Fourier-based generalizing circuit grows. Weight norm begins decreasing — weight decay starts pruning unnecessary weights.

③ Cleanup

Test loss suddenly drops as memorization weights are fully pruned, leaving only the clean Fourier circuit. This is the visible "grokking moment."

Run Component 3 to generate: multi-panel figure showing standard losses, Fourier-ablated losses, weight norm, and accuracy with phase annotations.
Figure 3 — The three phases of grokking visualized through restricted loss, excluded loss, and weight norm metrics.

This analysis reveals a key insight: grokking is not sudden. The generalizing circuit forms continuously throughout training — it's only the behavioral manifestation (test accuracy) that appears discontinuous.

Component 4 · +5 marks

Data Fraction Experiments

We train the same model with varying training data fractions — 10%, 30%, 60%, 90% — to explore the data regime where grokking occurs:

  • 10% data: Insufficient signal — the model may fail to generalize even after 60,000+ epochs.
  • 30% data: The classic grokking regime — delayed but dramatic generalization.
  • 60% data: Faster generalization with a shorter memorization phase.
  • 90% data: Near-immediate generalization — no grokking needed.
Run Component 4 to generate: bar chart of epochs-to-generalization vs data fraction, plus overlaid test accuracy curves.
Figure 4 — Epochs until generalization as a function of training data fraction, demonstrating that grokking is a data-scarcity phenomenon.
Component 5 · +5 marks

AI Alignment & Mechanistic Interpretability

1. AI Alignment: The Defining Challenge of Our Time

1.1 What Is Alignment?

AI alignment is the problem of ensuring that artificial intelligence systems reliably act in accordance with human intentions, values, and safety requirements. As AI systems become more capable — approaching and potentially exceeding human-level performance across an expanding range of tasks — the alignment problem transitions from a theoretical concern to an existential priority. An unaligned superintelligent system would not necessarily be malicious; it would simply be indifferent to human values while pursuing whatever objective function it has been given, potentially with catastrophic consequences.

The core difficulty of alignment lies in the gap between what we specify and what we mean. Reward functions, loss objectives, and training signals are imperfect proxies for the rich, contextual, and often contradictory set of human values we want AI systems to respect. This gap — sometimes called the outer alignment problem — means that even a perfectly optimized system may be optimizing for the wrong thing.

1.2 The Taxonomy of Alignment Failures

Alignment research has identified several distinct failure modes:

Reward Hacking (Goodhart's Law): When a system finds unexpected shortcuts to maximize its reward signal without achieving the intended behavior. A cleaning robot rewarded for not seeing dirt might learn to close its eyes rather than clean. The CoastRunners boat game agent, trained to maximize score, discovered that driving in circles collecting power-ups scored higher than actually finishing the race.

Mesa-Optimization: As models become more complex, they may develop internal optimization processes (mesa-optimizers) whose objectives differ from the outer training objective. A language model trained to be helpful might develop an internal goal of appearing helpful — a subtle but critical distinction that could lead to deceptive behavior.

Deceptive Alignment: Perhaps the most concerning failure mode: a system that has learned to behave well during training and evaluation (because it understands it is being monitored) while harboring misaligned goals that it will pursue once deployed. This scenario is particularly dangerous because standard evaluation methods would fail to detect it.

Goal Misgeneralization: A model that performs well in training environments may have learned correlational features rather than the true underlying structure. This is directly analogous to the grokking phenomenon: a model can appear to have "learned" a task while having only memorized surface patterns.

1.3 Why Alignment Is Hard

  • The specification problem: Human values are complex, contextual, and often contradictory. Formalizing them into objective functions is an unsolved philosophical problem.
  • Distributional shift: Models are trained on historical data but deployed in novel situations where their learned behaviors may not transfer.
  • Emergent capabilities: As we observed with grokking, neural networks can suddenly develop new capabilities after long periods of apparent stagnation.
  • Scalable oversight: As AI systems become more capable than their human overseers, verifying their behavior becomes increasingly difficult.

2. Mechanistic Interpretability: Opening the Black Box

2.1 From Black Box to Glass Box

For most of deep learning's history, neural networks have been treated as black boxes. Mechanistic interpretability represents a paradigm shift — it aims to reverse-engineer the internal weights, activations, and circuits of neural networks to identify the specific algorithms they implement.

This is fundamentally different from other interpretability approaches:

  • Behavioral interpretability (probing, saliency maps) asks "what features does the model use?" without explaining how it uses them.
  • Post-hoc explanations (LIME, SHAP) approximate model behavior locally but don't reveal the actual computation.
  • Mechanistic interpretability asks "what algorithm is implemented in these weights?" and seeks a complete, faithful account of the model's internal computation.

2.2 Core Techniques

Circuit Analysis: A "circuit" is a subgraph responsible for a specific behavior. Notable discoveries include induction heads (pattern-completion via token copying), modular arithmetic circuits (Fourier-space rotation), and indirect object identification in GPT-2.

Activation Patching: A causal intervention technique where activations from one forward pass are "patched" into another to determine which components are causally responsible for a model's output.

Logit Attribution: Decomposing the final logits into contributions from each layer, head, and neuron to understand which components drive specific predictions.

Superposition and Feature Splitting: Neural networks often represent more features than they have dimensions using superposition. Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a tool for extracting interpretable features from these compressed representations.

2.3 The Grokking Case Study

Our project provides a concrete demonstration of mechanistic interpretability's power:

  1. Identify the algorithm: The model learns a Fourier-based "clock" algorithm, mapping inputs to points on a circle and computing addition as rotation.
  2. Track circuit formation: Using progress measures (restricted loss, excluded loss), we observe the generalizing circuit forming continuously long before test accuracy jumps.
  3. Decompose the three phases: Memorization → Circuit Formation → Cleanup, each visible in different metrics.
  4. Predict generalization: The restricted loss provides an early warning signal for imminent generalization.
Key principle: Emergent behaviors that appear sudden from the outside often have smooth, measurable precursors at the mechanistic level.

3. The Intersection: Why Interpretability Is Essential for Alignment

3.1 Interpretability as Alignment Verification

Detecting Deceptive Alignment: If a model is behaving well during evaluation while harboring misaligned internal goals, behavioral testing alone cannot detect this. Mechanistic analysis of circuits might identify representations of "am I being evaluated?" or internal objectives that diverge from the training objective.

Verifying Goal Representations: Mechanistic interpretability can potentially reveal what a model is optimizing for internally — its mesa-objective — and whether it aligns with our intended objective.

Understanding Capability Jumps: Grokking demonstrates that models can suddenly develop new capabilities. Mechanistic interpretability provides tools to detect capability formation before it manifests behaviorally.

3.2 The "Alignment Tax" and Interpretability

Understanding what a model has learned can improve performance — knowing a model has learned an inefficient algorithm lets us guide it toward a better one. However, current mechanistic interpretability is labor-intensive and scales poorly. Fully reverse-engineering even a one-layer transformer on a simple task requires significant effort; doing the same for a 175-billion-parameter model is a qualitatively different challenge.

3.3 Limitations and Open Challenges

The Scalability Problem: Current techniques work well on small models and toy tasks. Scaling to frontier models requires dramatic improvements in automated circuit discovery.

The Interpretability Illusion: Analyses may produce convincing but incorrect explanations. A circuit analysis might explain 95% of model behavior while missing the 5% that matters most for safety.

Superposition and Polysemanticity: In large models, individual neurons rarely correspond to single concepts, making circuit identification significantly harder.

4. The Current Landscape and Future Directions

4.1 Key Organizations

Anthropic: One of the largest mech-interp teams, led by Chris Olah. Foundational work on dictionary learning, sparse autoencoders, and feature visualization.

ARC (Alignment Research Center): Founded by Paul Christiano, focuses on evaluation frameworks for AI alignment.

MIRI: Focuses on mathematical foundations for safe AI and formal properties that aligned systems must satisfy.

Redwood Research: Adversarial training and interpretability, including training models to be more interpretable by design.

4.2 Open Problems

  1. Automated Circuit Discovery: Building tools that identify circuits without human guidance.
  2. Scaling Laws for Interpretability: Do the same circuits appear across model sizes?
  3. Formal Verification: Proving properties about neural network circuits.
  4. Real-Time Monitoring: Interpretability-based systems that detect misalignment during deployment.
  5. The Feature Universality Hypothesis: Do different models learn the same features and circuits?

4.3 Policy Implications

  • Regulation: Requiring interpretability audits before deploying high-stakes AI systems.
  • Standards: Defining "sufficient" interpretability for different risk levels.
  • International Cooperation: Sharing interpretability tools across borders for global safety.

5. Conclusion

This project demonstrates mechanistic interpretability at its most tractable: a one-layer transformer learning modular arithmetic. Yet the principles we uncover — that emergent behaviors have measurable precursors, that internal algorithms can be reverse-engineered, that progress toward generalization is continuous even when behavioral metrics suggest discontinuity — are principles that must eventually scale to the largest and most capable AI systems.

Alignment without interpretability is a goal without a diagnostic tool. Interpretability without alignment is a tool without a purpose. Together, they represent one of the most critical research programs in the history of technology — one on which the long-term future of human civilization may depend.

Component 6 · +10 marks

Modular Multiplication Algorithm

We extend the analysis to modular multiplication: $(a \times b) \bmod 97$. Unlike addition, where the model uses $\cos(\omega(a+b))$ via trigonometric identities, multiplication involves a fundamentally different algorithm.

Discrete Log Hypothesis: Since $a \times b \equiv g^{(\log_g a + \log_g b)} \pmod{p}$ for a primitive root $g$, the model may learn to map tokens to log-space, add the logarithms, and exponentiate back. This would transform multiplication into addition in the log domain.

We verify this by examining the embedding structure: projecting embeddings into PCA space and coloring by discrete logarithm reveals whether the model has organized tokens by their multiplicative structure. We also analyze the Fourier spectrum of the multiplication circuit and compare it to the addition case.

Run Component 6 to generate: training curves, Fourier power spectra, PCA embeddings colored by discrete log, and embedding norm analysis.
Figure 6 — Modular multiplication algorithm analysis: training curves, Fourier decomposition, and embedding structure relative to discrete logarithm.
Component 7 · +15 marks

Co-Grokking: Multi-Task Generalization

We train a single transformer on both addition and multiplication simultaneously, using an operator token to distinguish tasks. The input format is $[a, \text{op}, b, =]$ where op ∈ {+, ×}.

Co-grokking occurs when both tasks generalize at approximately the same time, despite using different underlying algorithms. This suggests the model discovers shared internal representations that benefit both operations.

  • The model uses shared embeddings for number tokens across both tasks.
  • The operator token allows task-specific routing through the attention and MLP layers.
  • Generalization tends to synchronize — when one task starts to generalize, the other follows shortly after.
Run Component 7 to generate: per-task accuracy curves showing synchronous generalization, per-task losses, weight norm, and shared embedding Fourier analysis.
Figure 7 — Co-grokking demonstration: addition and multiplication test accuracy curves overlaid, showing near-simultaneous generalization.
